---
title: "R and RStudio Basics"
author: "DATA 614 - University of Calgary"
date: "March 3, 2026"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document which has been "knitted" as PDF. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word #documents in RStudio. You can include code, graphs, and so much more in these documents, and create professional-looking reports. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>. 

This document is for the Basics of R. 

# Indexing and selecting data 

## Using which()

```{r}
g=c(2,3,6,8,9,11)
which(g!=8) # >= <= < > ==
g[g!=8]
g[which(g!=8)]
```

Lets now consider dataframes. We can use a built-in dataset called "mtcars". We can use functions head(), tail(), and summary() to get to know the dataset:
```{r}
?mtcars

head(mtcars)
tail(mtcars)
summary(mtcars)

mtcars[,2]
mtcars[1,]


mtcars[,2]==6

which(mtcars[,2]==6)
# try this wrong one: mtcars[mtcars[,2]==6]
mtcars[mtcars[,2]==6,] 
mtcars[mtcars[,2]==6,1]

mtcars[which(mtcars[,2]==6),1]
mtcars[which(mtcars[,2]==6),2]

?class
class(mtcars)

class(mtcars[which(mtcars[,2]==6),2])

```

## Using $
To select a field of data from a data frame, we can also use the symbol $. 
```{r}
?mtcars

head(mtcars)
tail(mtcars)
summary(mtcars)

mtcars[,2]
mtcars$cyl
cbind(mtcars$cyl)

which(mtcars$cyl==6)
# try this wrong one: mtcars[mtcars[,2]==6]
mtcars[mtcars$cyl==6,] 
mtcars[mtcars$cyl==6,1]

mtcars$mpg[which(mtcars[,2]==6)]
mtcars$mpg[which(mtcars[,2]==6)]

```

# Importing data (CSV file)

```{r}
data<-read.csv(file = "patioSmall.csv",header = FALSE)

#install.packages("data.table",repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
library(data.table)

#practice
?fread
data<-fread("patioSmall.csv",col.names =c("Review","Rating","Heading","Month","Day","year")) 

summary(data)
View(data)

data[,2]
data[1,]
#your turn
data$Rating[data$Month==12]

mean(data$Rating[data$Month==12])
mean(data$Rating[data$Month==9])
```
# Working with qualitative data

Also known as categorical, entries are also known as factors or levels. Let's look at somethings that can be done on a dataset in the "MASS"" library called "painters".

```{r}
library(MASS)
?painters
painters

school = painters$School
school_freq = table(school)
school_freq

# can work on integers as well, but may not be useful
composition=painters$Composition
composition_freq = table(composition)
composition_freq

#exercise: find the school with most painters
which.max(school_freq) # doesn't give all maximums
which(school_freq==max(school_freq))

nrow(painters)

#exercise: find relative frequency distribution of schools
school_rel_freq=school_freq/nrow(painters)
school_rel_freq
```

# Working with quantitative data
We will use the built-in "airmiles" dataset for the quantitative data.
```{r}
?airmiles
airmiles 
range(airmiles) #find the range of data
range(painters$Composition)


breaks = seq(range(airmiles)[1], range(airmiles)[2],by =(range(airmiles)[2]-range(airmiles)[1])/10 )
breaks
?cut
airmiles.cut = cut(airmiles, breaks, right=FALSE)
airmiles.cut

airmiles.freq=table(airmiles.cut)
airmiles.freq

#exercise: calculate relative frequency
airmiles.rel.freq=airmiles.freq/sum(airmiles.freq)
airmiles.rel.freq

sum(airmiles.rel.freq)

#exercise: calculate cumulative frequency and relative frequency
airmiles.cum.freq=cumsum(airmiles.freq)
airmiles.cum.freq

airmiles.cum.rel.freq=cumsum(airmiles.rel.freq)
airmiles.cum.rel.freq


#put the frequency, cumulative frequency, relative frequency, and cumulative relative frequency next to each other in one table.
?options
options(digits = 2)
cbind(airmiles.freq,airmiles.cum.freq,airmiles.rel.freq,airmiles.cum.rel.freq)

#plot the cumulative frequencies
plot(breaks, c(0,airmiles.cum.freq))
lines(breaks, c(0,airmiles.cum.freq))

plot(breaks, c(0,airmiles.cum.freq),xlab="Airmiles",ylab = "Cumulative frequency")
lines(breaks, c(0,airmiles.cum.freq))

```

There are also basic measurements that you can do on numerical data:
```{r}
#mean

mean(mtcars$mpg)
#exercise: how to calculate the mean(mtcars$cyl)
as.numeric(levels(mtcars$cyl)[mtcars$cyl])
mean(as.numeric(levels(mtcars$cyl)[mtcars$cyl]))

#median
median(mtcars$mpg)

#quartiles
quantile(mtcars$mpg)
quantile(mtcars$mpg,c(.12,.98))

#range
range(mtcars$mpg)
max(mtcars$mpg)-min(mtcars$mpg)
range(mtcars$mpg)[2]-range(mtcars$mpg)[1]

#interquartile range
IQR(mtcars$mpg)
quantile(mtcars$mpg)[[4]]-quantile(mtcars$mpg)[[2]]
dim(quantile(mtcars$mpg))
typeof(quantile(mtcars$mpg))
class(quantile(mtcars$mpg))

#variance and standard deviation
var(mtcars$mpg)
sd(mtcars$mpg)

#covariance and correlation coefficient
cov(mtcars$mpg,mtcars$disp)
cor(mtcars$mpg,mtcars$disp)

cov(mtcars$disp,mtcars$hp)
cor(mtcars$disp,mtcars$hp)

```

# Dealing with missing values
```{r}

x1 <- c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5, 6)

x1 == NA


is.na(x1)

x2=x1[!is.na(x1)]
x2

x1 < 0

is.na(x1) <- which(x1 == 6)
x1

x3 <- c("a", "B", NA, "NA")
# NA is different from "NA"
is.na(x3)

x <- c(1,2,NA,3)
mean(x) 
mean(x, na.rm=TRUE) 



# NaN is different from NA. Try this:
0/0

dataNA=read.csv(file = "patioSmallNA.csv",header = FALSE)

newdataNA=dataNA[!complete.cases(dataNA),]

dataNA = na.omit(dataNA)

is.na(dataNA)
dataNA2=dataNA[!complete.cases(dataNA),]
#dataNA2
newdataNA = na.omit(dataNA)
#newdataNA
```
